name: Hank Crawford
abstract: Bennie Ross "Hank" Crawford, Jr. (December 21, 1934 – January 29, 2009) was an American alto saxophonist, pianist, arranger and songwriter whose genres ranged from R&B, hard bop, jazz-funk, and soul jazz.

about_quotes.speaker: Jimmy Owens
about_quotes.evidence: After I left, I started to work with Hank Crawford... I had my nine months or so experience working with Hank Crawford. Musically, it was wonderful.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jimmy Owens — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Hubert Laws
about_quotes.evidence: George Benson, Bob James, Ron Carter, Jack Dejohnette ... Hank Crawford, Johnny Hammond. Esther Phillips was there too, the first tour.
about_quotes.doc_title: Hubert Laws — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Dick Griffin
about_quotes.evidence: Hank Crawford had a style of making an eight piece group sound like a big band. He got that, he learned that from my teacher whose name is Mr. William W. Davis.
about_quotes.doc_title: Dick Griffin interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, February 12, 2024

about_quotes.speaker: Derrick Gardner
about_quotes.evidence: My father is a jazz trumpeter and he was in the Ray Charles band back in the early sixties, with Hank Crawford and Marcus Belgrade and all those guys
about_quotes.doc_title: Derrick Gardner interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, May 9, 2013

about_quotes.speaker: Joel Dorn
about_quotes.evidence: I had some new ideas for Hank Crawford... I threw her in with like real guys: Kenny Barron, Ron Carter, Lewis Nash, Fathead, Hank Crawford.
about_quotes.doc_title: Joel Dorn interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 13, 2007

about_quotes.speaker: Jane Monheit
about_quotes.evidence: having Kenny Barron, Ron Carter and Lewis Nash play on my first album. Bucky Pizzarelli, you know, Fathead, Hank Crawford.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jane Monheit interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, August 20, 2010
